summary: Mary Midgley's philosophy emphasizes the intrinsic relationality of human beings, critiquing atomistic individualism that views people as isolated, egoistic units. She argues that our evolutionary history proves we are deeply social animals, and this interconnectedness extends beyond our species to include non-human animals and the broader biosphere (Gaia).

principles.statement: Humans are fundamentally social and relational animals, not isolated, egoistic atoms.
principles.rationale: Our evolutionary history shows we developed from social creatures. Our intellect and language are outgrowths of our innate sociability, not the inventors of it.
principles.source_quote: Before we are thinkers, we are lovers and haters, creatures deeply aware of those around us and fully integrated into their life.

principles.statement: We must recognize that we are animals, and our moral community naturally includes non-human animals.
principles.rationale: Humans have an innate impulse to connect with the world, leading to the domestication and integration of animals into our social lives. Animals are fellow subjects capable of deep relationships.
principles.source_quote: we are not just rather like animals; we are animals

principles.statement: Our imaginative visions of the world shape our moral principles and actions.
principles.rationale: How we picture human sociality and our place in the world dictates our priorities and duties. If we see ourselves as part of a larger interdependent whole, we will care for it.
principles.source_quote: When this larger imaginative vision changes, the light in which we see all our various concerns is altered. Priorities shift, carrying a corresponding change in duties.

mental_models.name: The Dance
mental_models.description: A metaphor for human sociality where individual lives are not isolated performances but parts of a larger, intricate choreography. The soloist and the dance need and complete each other.
mental_models.example: Viewing our lives not as a collection of solo performances but an immensely intricate large-scale dance in which solos take their place among figures performed by groups of varying sizes.

mental_models.name: The Tree / Organism
mental_models.description: A holistic lens viewing individuals as distinct parts of a larger, interdependent system, rather than disconnected objects like billiard balls.
mental_models.example: Understanding that a person receives life in a family (as a petal does in a flower), in a country (as the flower does on the tree), and in the biosphere (as the tree does in the forest).

mental_models.name: Gaia
mental_models.description: Viewing the Earth and its life as an active, self-maintaining, interdependent whole, rather than a loose jumble of competing entities.
mental_models.example: Using Gaia as a 'good myth' to understand our parental relationship to the earth and our vulnerability within its systems, countering the hubristic view that humans are at the center of the cosmos.

heuristics: Look at human sociability zoologically, as Darwin did, rather than strictly intellectually.

signature_quotes.text: Before we are thinkers, we are lovers and haters, creatures deeply aware of those around us and fully integrated into their life.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining that human intellect is an outgrowth of our innate evolutionary sociability.

signature_quotes.text: we are not just rather like animals; we are animals
signature_quotes.context: The foundational statement of her philosophy locating humans alongside other animals in the natural world.

signature_quotes.text: Of course, our dependencies are dangerous, but who wants to live safely like a billiard ball or a doll that never leaves its package?
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing the Stoic and Western philosophical tendency to view social bonds and dependencies as weaknesses.

signature_quotes.text: It may even become possible for our species to admit that it is not really a supernatural variety of Lego, but some kind of an animal.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing against the idea that humans can easily snap in and out of each other's lives without consequence.

signature_quotes.text: The self’s wholeness is not, then, the wholeness of a billiard ball but that of an organism, a transient, struggling creature which has, of course, its own distinct shape but which still belongs in its own context and background.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the integrated self within its wider ecological and social systems.

anti_patterns.description: Social Atomism / Egoistic Individualism (The Hobbesian view)
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores our evolutionary history as social primates, misconstrues relationships as merely provisional or instrumental, and fragments our understanding of the self by treating humans as discrete, self-contained units like billiard balls.
It obscures the recognition of animal subjectivity and relationality, ignoring the deep, complex social bonds humans naturally form with animals by reducing them to mere objects.
